audit_text: **`PATCH /api/sessions/{id}` with `tag_ids` skips `_validate_tag_cardinality`.** POST validates; PATCH does not. Bulk-replace via PATCH can permanently land a 2-project state. **Same bug as #1 finding 2 — independently surfaced by two reviewers.**
spec_anchor: docs/behavior/sessions.md (no §Tags section); enforcement intent per src/bearings/web/routes/sessions.py:161-212 _validate_tag_cardinality docstring + V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md §P0 item 7
verdict_evidence: routes/sessions.py PATCH handler (L435-516) validates tag existence (L459-473) and replaces the set via tags_db.set_for_session (L510), but never calls _validate_tag_cardinality. The validator is invoked only inside create_session (L371). db/tags.py set_for_session (L643-667) likewise does not validate cardinality — it just DELETEs and re-INSERTs join rows. The validator's own docstring (L168-174) names POST + set_for_session as bulk-replace paths but the call only lands in POST.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /api/sessions/{id} with tag_ids carrying 2 project-class tags returns HTTP 422 with detail describing the project-class violation.
PATCH /api/sessions/{id} with tag_ids carrying 2 severity-class tags returns HTTP 422 with detail describing the severity-class violation.
PATCH /api/sessions/{id} with a valid mixed tag_ids set (≤1 project, ≤1 severity, any number of general) returns 200 and the new set is persisted.
_validate_tag_cardinality is invoked before tags_db.set_for_session in routes/sessions.py:patch_session.
A regression test in tests/ exercises the 422 path (project + severity violation cases).
executor_brief: PATCH /api/sessions/{id} replaces the session's tag set via set_for_session but skips the cardinality guard that POST runs. Add a call to _validate_tag_cardinality(db, new_tag_ids) inside patch_session before the set_for_session call (after existence-check, before persistence). Mirror the POST-side error shape (422 with the structured detail). Add tests covering 2-project and 2-severity violations and a happy-path mixed-class case.

audit_text: **`%` and `_` in search input unescaped before LIKE.** `100%` matches anything starting with `100`.
spec_anchor: docs/behavior/sessions.md (no search section — search behavior is in routes/history.py module docstring + V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md §P1 item 12)
verdict_evidence: routes/history.py:_search builds the LIKE pattern at L102 as `pattern = f"%{q}%"` with no escaping of literal `%` or `_` characters in `q`. Both queries (L107-118 sessions, L140-150 messages) declare `ESCAPE '\\'` but the user-supplied `q` is interpolated raw. Querying `100%` therefore turns into `LIKE '%100%%'` and matches any text containing `100` followed by anything; `_` likewise still acts as a wildcard.
acceptance_criteria: Searching `100%` only returns sessions/messages whose text contains the literal substring `100%` (not strings starting with `100`).
Searching `foo_bar` only returns sessions/messages whose text contains the literal substring `foo_bar` (not `foo` + any character + `bar`).
Searching `\\` (a literal backslash) is still findable when the user types it — the escape character is not consumed unintentionally.
history.py escapes `\\`, `%`, and `_` in `q` before interpolating into the LIKE pattern (in that order so the backslash escape is applied first).
A regression test in tests/test_history_search.py covers the `100%`, `foo_bar`, and literal-backslash inputs.
executor_brief: User input flows raw into the LIKE pattern despite the ESCAPE clause being declared. Add a private `_escape_like(q: str) -> str` helper that backslash-escapes `\\`, `%`, and `_` in that order, call it on `q.strip()` before building `pattern = f"%{escaped}%"`. Keep the ESCAPE '\\' clause as-is. Add tests for `100%`, `foo_bar`, and literal-backslash inputs.

audit_text: **`PUT/DELETE /api/sessions/{sid}/tags/{tid}` does not call `publish_upsert`.** Tag-only mutations invisible to other tabs. WS stream shows nothing.
spec_anchor: docs/architecture-v1.md §1.1.5 — sessions broadcaster fan-out invariant; V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md §P1 item 13 (CCW-3)
verdict_evidence: routes/tags.py attach_tag (L257-275) calls tags_db.attach then re-fetches the tag and returns; never imports SessionsBroadcaster, never reads request.app.state.sessions_broadcaster, never invokes publish_upsert/publish_delete. detach_tag (L282-290) likewise just calls tags_db.detach and returns 204. Other tabs subscribed to /ws/sessions therefore receive no event when a tag is added or removed via these endpoints.
acceptance_criteria: PUT /api/sessions/{sid}/tags/{tid} broadcasts a session_upsert frame for `sid` after the attach succeeds.
DELETE /api/sessions/{sid}/tags/{tid} broadcasts a session_upsert frame for `sid` after the detach succeeds.
The broadcast carries the freshly-fetched SessionOut for `sid` (so subscribers see the updated tag set on the row).
Idempotent attach (already-attached) still emits the upsert (or skips it deliberately — document the choice in the route docstring).
A regression test in tests/ subscribes a fake broadcaster, calls PUT and DELETE, and asserts a session_upsert frame is fanned out for each.
executor_brief: The per-session attach/detach endpoints mutate session_tags but never broadcast. Add a SessionsBroadcaster lookup via getattr(request.app.state, 'sessions_broadcaster', None) (mirroring routes/sessions.py:_sessions_broadcaster), refetch the SessionOut after the mutation, and call publish_upsert when broadcaster is not None. Apply to both attach_tag and detach_tag. Test with a fake broadcaster recording publish calls.

audit_text: **Tag CRUD also does not broadcast** (`POST/PATCH/DELETE /api/tags`, sort-order). Filter panels in other tabs go stale.
verdict_evidence: routes/tags.py POST create_tag (L93-116), PATCH update_tag (L193-219), PATCH /pinned (L222-233), DELETE delete_tag (L236-242), and PUT /api/tags/sort-order (L148-167) all return after the DB call without invoking any broadcaster. ws_sessions.py exposes only session_upsert/session_delete/runner_state frame types (L54-56) — there is no tag-channel broadcast surface yet, so no broadcast is emitted from any tag-CRUD path. A second tab's TagFilterPanel sees stale data until the user manually reloads.
acceptance_criteria: POST /api/tags broadcasts a tag_upsert frame after the row is inserted.
PATCH /api/tags/{id}, PATCH /api/tags/{id}/pinned, and PUT /api/tags/sort-order each broadcast a tag_upsert frame for the affected tag(s).
DELETE /api/tags/{id} broadcasts a tag_delete frame carrying the tag_id.
ws_sessions.py (or a dedicated ws_tags.py) defines tag_upsert / tag_delete frame types and a SessionsBroadcaster.publish_tag_* surface mirroring publish_upsert/publish_delete.
frontend/src/lib/stores/tags.svelte.ts subscribes to the new frame types and updates the in-memory tag list without polling.
Regression tests in tests/ assert each route emits the expected frame to a fake broadcaster.
executor_brief: Tag CRUD endpoints mutate the tag table without telling other tabs. Extend SessionsBroadcaster (or split out a TagsBroadcaster) with publish_tag_upsert(tag) and publish_tag_delete(tag_id), wire all five tag-mutation routes to call them, and update the frontend tags store to consume the new frames. Behaviour-wise the broadcast must arrive before the response, mirroring existing session-broadcast invariants.

audit_text: **Single-attach cardinality hole.** User can permanently hold 2 project tags with no schema guard and no path that forces correction.
verdict_evidence: PUT /api/sessions/{sid}/tags/{tid} (routes/tags.py:257-275) calls tags_db.attach with no cardinality check. The single-attach exemption is documented in the validator docstring (sessions.py:168-174) on the assumption 'the next bulk replace will reject' — but finding 1 confirms PATCH bulk-replace ALSO skips the validator. The combination means a user who attaches a second project tag via PUT lands in a 2-project state and no API path can detect or correct it (no schema CHECK either, per db/schema.sql:169 comment 'API boundary enforces cardinality'). DB-level cardinality is genuinely unenforced.
acceptance_criteria: PUT /api/sessions/{sid}/tags/{tid} returns 422 when the resulting set would carry >1 project-class tag (or >1 severity-class tag).
Validation runs against the existing attached set + the new tag (a SELECT to load the existing class set, then re-use _validate_tag_cardinality on the would-be new tuple).
Idempotent re-attach of the same tag still returns 200.
Combined with finding 1 fix, the cardinality invariant is unbreakable through the public API.
Regression test attaches one project tag (passes), then attempts a second project tag via PUT (asserts 422).
executor_brief: Decide-and-move-on: drop the single-attach exemption — fix together with finding 1 so cardinality is enforced everywhere (POST + PATCH + PUT). In attach_tag, load the current attached tag classes via tags_db.list_for_session, append the new id's class, and call _validate_tag_cardinality on the union before persisting. Keep idempotency: re-attaching an already-attached tag remains a 200 no-op.

audit_text: `tag_ids_other` wire param vs `class=general` in DB — rename to `tag_ids_general` before param ossifies.
spec_anchor: docs/architecture-v1.md (wire-shape consistency); V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md §P2 item 21 (treated as design observation)
verdict_evidence: Backend list_sessions (routes/sessions.py:258) accepts `tag_ids_other`; db/sessions.py:list_all (L470, 563-570) consumes the same name; frontend api/sessions.ts:283 emits `tag_ids_other`; integration tests assert it. Nowhere does the codebase name the third bucket `general`, despite the DB column being `tags.class = 'general'` (db/tags.py:131 TAG_CLASS_GENERAL). The wire/DB mismatch persists and the param has not been renamed.
acceptance_criteria: All references to `tag_ids_other` in route signatures, DB query helpers, frontend API client, stores, tests, and OpenAPI export use `tag_ids_general` instead.
`docs/openapi.json` is regenerated as part of the same commit (per CLAUDE.md regeneration discipline).
Old param name is NOT preserved as a back-compat alias — pre-1.0 codebase per CLAUDE.md SemVer policy; document the rename in CHANGELOG.
Integration tests still pass with the new param name.
executor_brief: Pure rename task across backend route param, DB helper, frontend API client, store, and tests. Drop the legacy name (pre-1.0, no back-compat needed). Regenerate docs/openapi.json in the same commit. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] noting the rename.

audit_text: History search does not hit tags — a session tagged "Bearings v1" won't surface when you type "Bearings v1" unless it's also in title/description.
verdict_evidence: routes/history.py:_search runs two queries (L107-118 sessions; L140-150 messages). Both filter on session/message text columns only. There is no JOIN through session_tags / tags. A session whose title and message bodies do not contain the search term will not surface even when the user is searching by tag name.
acceptance_criteria: Searching for a tag name returns sessions tagged with that tag (in addition to title/description hits).
Tag-name hits are presented as a third hit kind or merged with session hits — choose and document.
Result cap discipline preserved: tag hits compete for the same HISTORY_SEARCH_RESULT_CAP slots; sessions take priority.
Regression test creates a session, attaches a uniquely-named tag, searches for the tag name, asserts the session appears.
executor_brief: Add a third query to _search joining session_tags + tags on a name LIKE :pat predicate, returning the parent sessions. Decide kind labelling (recommend new `tag` kind so the UI can render a tag chip; this lines up with finding 13). Add ESCAPE-aware LIKE (depends on finding 2 fix landing in the same module).

audit_text: No FTS5 — bare LIKE on `messages.content` is a full-table scan on every keystroke.
verdict_evidence: routes/history.py message query at L140-150 runs `WHERE m.content LIKE :pat`. db/schema.sql defines no FTS5 virtual table; grep for `fts5\|virtual table` across the schema returns nothing. Every search performs a full scan of messages.content; with HISTORY_SEARCH_DEBOUNCE_MS the cost is paid per keystroke.
acceptance_criteria: schema.sql adds an FTS5 virtual table over messages(content) with appropriate triggers to keep it in sync on insert/update/delete.
history.py message query uses the FTS5 MATCH operator instead of LIKE for the content column.
Existing fresh installs build the FTS index from the schema; a migration backfills the index on existing DBs.
Search latency on a populated DB (≥10k messages) is bounded by the FTS index lookup, not a full scan.
Regression test exercises FTS-tokenizer-specific behaviour (phrase queries, stemming if enabled) so future schema drift is caught.
executor_brief: Add an FTS5 virtual table mirroring messages(content) plus AFTER INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE triggers. Rewrite the message-side query in history.py to MATCH against the FTS table, joining back to messages by rowid. Add a migration that builds the index for existing rows. Document the tokenizer choice (default unicode61 is sufficient) in schema.sql.

audit_text: **`_is_known_model` duplicated in `db/tags.py` and `db/templates.py`.** A `db/_validators.py` module solves without cross-import cycle.
verdict_evidence: Commit 8d10474e ("refactor(db): extract _is_known_model into db/_validators.py") created src/bearings/db/_validators.py as the single source of truth. db/tags.py:84 and db/templates.py:51 both `from bearings.db._validators import _is_known_model`. The local duplicates the audit cited are gone. The commit message explicitly references V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md feature 5 finding 9. Note: db/sessions.py:207 and db/routing.py:60 retain their own local `_is_known_model` defs, but those were not the duplications the audit named — they belong to the broader CCW-4 work-stream and are out of scope for this finding.

audit_text: `SessionsBroadcaster._fan_out` iterates the live set — no `await` mid-loop keeps it safe now but one future reentrant caller breaks it. Snapshot with `list()`.
spec_anchor: docs/architecture-v1.md §1.1.5 (broadcaster invariants); V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md §P1 item 13 / §P2 item 21 hardening
verdict_evidence: ws_sessions.py:_fan_out (L127-135) iterates `self._subscribers` directly with `for q in self._subscribers`. The set is mutated by subscribe/unsubscribe (L78-84). Today `put_nowait` is synchronous so no await intervenes — but the audit's point is robustness against a future reentrant change. No `list(self._subscribers)` snapshot is taken.
acceptance_criteria: _fan_out iterates `list(self._subscribers)` (or a tuple snapshot) so a concurrent unsubscribe during fan-out cannot raise RuntimeError.
Existing fan-out tests still pass.
A regression test (or comment) documents the reentrant-safety rationale so the snapshot isn't 'optimised away' later.
executor_brief: Replace `for q in self._subscribers` with `for q in list(self._subscribers)` (or `tuple(self._subscribers)`) in _fan_out. One-line change. Add a brief docstring/comment explaining the snapshot is for future-reentrant-caller safety, not current behaviour.

audit_text: Subscriber queues unbounded — a hung tab OOMs the server eventually.
verdict_evidence: subscribe (L76-80) creates `asyncio.Queue[str]()` with no maxsize. `_fan_out` (L127-135) calls `put_nowait` whose docstring (L131-133) acknowledges 'queues are unbounded ... put_nowait never raises QueueFull here.' A subscriber that stops draining accumulates frames indefinitely; a hung browser tab → unbounded memory growth.
acceptance_criteria: Subscriber queues are created with a maxsize from a config constant (e.g. `SESSIONS_BROADCAST_QUEUE_MAX`).

When `put_nowait` raises QueueFull (slow/hung subscriber), the broadcaster either (a) drops oldest then puts, or (b) drops the slow subscriber by closing its websocket — choose and document the policy.
The chosen policy is covered by a regression test that fills a queue beyond cap and asserts the documented behaviour.
Existing fan-out tests still pass; in particular, healthy subscribers continue to receive frames.
executor_brief: Decide-and-move-on: cap the subscriber queue at a sane size (recommend 256 frames, lives in config/constants.py) and on QueueFull drop the slow subscriber (close the websocket via a registered close-callback rather than silently dropping frames — silent drops would violate completeness). Add a regression test. Update the _fan_out docstring to reflect the new policy.

audit_text: `set_for_session` and `update_sort_orders` rely on implicit transaction isolation, not explicit BEGIN/COMMIT via aiosqlite context manager.
verdict_evidence: set_for_session (L643-667) issues a DELETE then a loop of INSERTs and finishes with `await connection.commit()`. update_sort_orders (L706-759) issues a SELECT, then UPDATEs in a loop, then `await connection.commit()`. Neither wraps in `async with connection:` (aiosqlite's transaction CM) nor opens an explicit `BEGIN`. Atomicity relies on aiosqlite's default isolation_level=DEFERRED auto-starting on the first DML — fine when no concurrent writers exist, but the docstring's 'one BEGIN ... COMMIT' claim (L651-653, L714-715) is aspirational.
acceptance_criteria: set_for_session opens an explicit transaction (`async with connection.execute('BEGIN')` followed by COMMIT, OR `async with connection:` if aiosqlite's CM is preferred — choose and document).
update_sort_orders does the same.
On exception mid-loop, the implicit/explicit ROLLBACK is verified — partial state is impossible.
Docstrings updated to reflect the explicit transaction semantics (drop the 'implicit BEGIN' aspirational language).
executor_brief: Wrap both helpers in explicit transactions. Pattern: `await connection.execute('BEGIN')` at entry; `await connection.commit()` on success; `await connection.rollback()` on exception (re-raise). Or use `async with connection:` if aiosqlite's connection CM commits on success and rolls back on exception (verify against aiosqlite version pin before committing). Update docstrings.

audit_text: SidebarSearch: no aria-live region, no nav hint footer, no timestamps or tag chips in results, no cap indicator.
verdict_evidence: Reading the .svelte template: the dialog uses `role=dialog` + `aria-modal=true` + `role=listbox` for results, but no `aria-live` region. No footer with keyboard-navigation hints. Each result row renders kind label + title + snippet only (L212-225) — no created_at timestamp, no tag chip, no per-row metadata beyond text. No element conveys the result cap (e.g. 'Showing top 50 of 500+ matches'). All four UX gaps are present.
acceptance_criteria: An `aria-live=polite` region announces the result count after a debounced search settles (e.g. '12 results' / 'No results' / 'Showing top 50, more available').
A footer row inside the dialog renders nav hints (`↑↓ navigate`, `Enter open`, `Esc close`) — string table additions in SIDEBAR_SEARCH_STRINGS.
Each result row renders the parent session's last-updated timestamp (relative format) and the attached tag chips.
When `results.length === HISTORY_SEARCH_RESULT_CAP`, a cap-indicator row appears at the bottom (e.g. 'Showing top 50 — refine query to see more').
All four additions are covered by SidebarSearch.test.ts assertions.
executor_brief: Four discrete UI/a11y additions to SidebarSearch.svelte. (1) Add `<div aria-live=polite class="sr-only">` updated whenever results change. (2) Render a footer row inside .sidebar-search-dialog. (3) Backend already returns session_id and message_id; the frontend needs to enrich result rows with timestamps + tags via either an API change or a follow-up fetch — recommend extending HistorySearchResult to include `updated_at` and `tag_ids`, populated server-side. (4) Compare results.length to the cap constant and render an indicator row. Add tests for each.

audit_text: `tag.color` populated in DB and wire shape but unused in `SessionRow` chip rendering.
spec_anchor: frontend/src/lib/components/sidebar/SessionRow.svelte; src/bearings/db/tags.py Tag.color column docstring; V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md §P2 item 21
verdict_evidence: Tag.color is a populated column (db/tags.py:124, validation at L141-144) and surfaces on the wire in TagOut (web/routes/tags.py:79). SessionRow.svelte renders the chip with utility classes only — `bg-accent` / `bg-surface-2` / `text-fg-strong` / `text-fg-muted` (L719-723) — and never reads `tag.color`. The user can save a custom colour but the sidebar chip ignores it.
acceptance_criteria: When `tag.color` is non-null, the chip renders with that colour (background or border — choose and document).
When `tag.color` is null, the chip falls back to the existing utility-class colours.
The selected/active state styling (currently `bg-accent`) still differentiates a filter-set tag chip even when a custom color is set.
Color contrast is acceptable for both light and dark themes (or document a known limitation).
Regression test renders SessionRow with a tag carrying `color: '#ff0080'` and asserts the chip element's inline-style background uses the colour.
executor_brief: Two-line change in the chip render block: add `style={tag.color ? `background-color: ${tag.color}` : undefined}` to the chip span. Decide whether selected-tag styling (currently `bg-accent`) overrides or composes with the custom colour. Add a test rendering a coloured tag chip.
